evals.prompt: I'm starting a new skill and need a seed doc on deploying containerized services to a serverless platform. Use these two pages -- https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/overview/what-is-cloud-run and https://cloud.google.com/run/docs/deploying -- plus these internal notes: we run one service per component, images come from a container registry built by our CI pipeline, and production services pin min-instances to 1. Write it to serverless-deploy-seed.md.
evals.expected_output: Happy path, mixed sources. The skill confirms the brief, routes both URLs to the web adapter and the notes to pasted-text, shows the resolved source list for confirmation before fetching, retrieves the two pages, and writes serverless-deploy-seed.md. The document has the metadata header (Status: Draft (generated), purpose, generated date, source count), a Purpose & scope section, a themed body that MERGES the public serverless concepts with the internal deployment notes (not one section per source), [S1]-[S3] provenance tags, an Open questions & gaps section, and a Sources table listing the two URLs and the notes with retrieval dates. No facts invented beyond the three sources.
expectations: A file named serverless-deploy-seed.md is created.
The document opens with a metadata header containing Status, Purpose, a Generated date, and a source count.
The Status field reads 'Draft (generated)'.
The document contains both a 'Purpose & scope' section and an 'Open questions & gaps' section.
The Sources section lists exactly three sources -- the two documentation URLs and the internal notes -- each with a retrieval or provided date.
No sources beyond the three the user supplied appear in the Sources list (nothing invented or added).
Inline provenance tags such as [S1], [S2], [S3] appear in the body, and every tag maps to an entry in the Sources list.
The internal notes (one service per component, registry images, min-instances 1) are woven into thematic sections rather than confined to a single notes-dump section.
Before fetching, the skill shows the resolved source list and waits for the user's confirmation.
Claims attributed to the documentation pages are supported by those pages and are not contradicted or fabricated.

evals.prompt: /research -- I want to build a new skill about our internal reference-data lookup service.
evals.expected_output: Under-specified invocation, tests elicitation. Because no sources or scope were given, the skill does NOT start fetching, synthesizing, or writing. It prompts for the brief -- topic/purpose confirmation, intended use & audience, scope (in and out), the sources to synthesize (web pages, local files, pasted notes, or any installed extension sources), and the desired output filename/location -- then waits for the user. It does not fabricate service details from prior knowledge.
expectations: No seed document file is created during this turn.
The skill asks the user for the brief before performing any research or retrieval.
The skill explicitly asks the user to provide the sources to synthesize.
The skill asks about scope and the intended use/audience.
The skill asks for the desired output filename or location.
The skill does not retrieve any web pages and does not state facts about the service drawn from its own prior knowledge.

evals.prompt: Pull together a seed doc for a skill about our data-feed format. The authoritative spec lives in our internal wiki at WIKI/Feed Spec, and there's also the public XML Schema primer at https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema11-1/. Output feed-format-seed.md.
evals.expected_output: Unavailable adapter, tests graceful degradation. Run this WITHOUT an internal-wiki extension installed. The skill routes the W3C URL to the web adapter. For the internal-wiki reference it finds no available adapter (no matching file under references/sources/ and no corresponding MCP tool), so it says so plainly and asks how to proceed -- e.g. paste the page content or install/enable a wiki extension -- rather than inventing the spec. If the user opts to continue with just the reachable source, it produces feed-format-seed.md from the W3C page, with an Open questions & gaps entry noting the internal Feed Spec was unavailable, and a Sources table listing only what was actually retrieved.
expectations: The W3C URL (https://www.w3.org/TR/xmlschema11-1/) is routed to the web adapter and retrieved.
The skill explicitly tells the user that no adapter is available for the internal-wiki source (no wiki extension or MCP tool is present).
The skill does not fabricate or invent contents for the 'WIKI/Feed Spec' page.
The skill asks the user how to proceed about the unavailable source (for example: paste the content, or install/enable a wiki extension).
If a seed document is produced, its Sources list includes only sources actually retrieved (the W3C page) and excludes the internal-wiki page.
If a seed document is produced, its Open questions & gaps section notes that the internal Feed Spec was unavailable.

evals.prompt: I've attached an internal operations runbook. Turn it into a seed doc for an ops skill -- I want the operational procedures captured as a reference. Save it as runbook-ops-seed.md.
evals.expected_output: Tests the local-file adapter. The skill reads the attached file (extracting text appropriately for its type rather than dumping raw bytes), confirms scope, and writes runbook-ops-seed.md -- themed by procedure/topic, with [S1] provenance, a Sources entry for the file (name, type, retrieved date), and an Open questions & gaps section for anything the runbook leaves implicit. When running this case, attach any real spec or runbook (PDF, DOCX, or MD) as the source; the filename in files is a placeholder to be swapped for the attachment.
expectations: The attached file is read and its text extracted appropriately (not dumped as raw bytes).
A file named runbook-ops-seed.md is created.
The body is organised by procedure/topic (themed), not as a single raw dump of the file.
Inline [S1] provenance tags appear and map to a Sources entry for the file (name, type, retrieved date).
The document includes an 'Open questions & gaps' section for anything the runbook leaves implicit.
